_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Hale County, AL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Hale County (formed 1867 from Greene and Marengo counties, seat at Greensboro) is a Reconstruction-era creation — pre-1867 records for this territory are in Greene County and Marengo County courthouses.

previous_courthouses.fate: Served from the county's Reconstruction-era formation until replaced by the 1908 building. Records transferred.

record_losses.cause: County created in 1867 — earlier records held by parent counties
record_losses.scope: none for Hale County records; pre-1867 records at Greene and Marengo
record_losses.description: Hale County was created in 1867 from parts of Greene County and Marengo County. Records before 1867 — deeds, wills, marriages, court minutes — remain in the parent county courthouses at Eutaw (Greene) and Linden (Marengo). Hale County's own records begin in 1867. No major fire or war damage has affected the Hale County courthouse.
record_losses.narrative_impact: For {ancestor_name} in what is now Hale County before 1867, the records are at Eutaw (Greene County) or Linden (Marengo County), not Greensboro. The jurisdictional split during Reconstruction means the paper trail crosses county lines at 1867.
record_losses.source: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from formation. Pre-1867 deeds at Greene and Marengo counties.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps in Hale County records. The critical research challenge is knowing which parent county held jurisdiction for the specific property before 1867.
deeds.repository: Hale County Probate Office; ADAH microfilm; Greene County (Eutaw) and Marengo County (Linden) for pre-1867

wills_estates.completeness: Good from formation. Pre-1867 estate records at Greene and Marengo.
wills_estates.gaps: None significant for the Hale County period.
wills_estates.repository: Hale County Probate Office; ADAH; parent counties for pre-1867

marriages.completeness: Good from formation. Includes freedmen's marriages from the county's earliest year.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1867 marriages at Greene and Marengo.
marriages.repository: Hale County Probate Office; ADAH; Freedmen's Bureau at NARA

vital_records.repository: Alabama Center for Health Statistics; ADAH

substitute_sources.source_type: Greene County and Marengo County records (pre-1867)
substitute_sources.description: All records for the territory now constituting Hale County prior to 1867 are in the parent county courthouses.
substitute_sources.repository: Greene County Probate Office (Eutaw); Marengo County Probate Office (Linden); ADAH
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing Hale County families before the county's creation. Researchers must search both parent counties to determine which held jurisdiction for a specific property or individual.
substitute_sources.source: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.

substitute_sources.description: Bureau records from the Greensboro area document formerly enslaved families.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for the slavery-to-freedom transition. Hale County's formation (1867) coincides with the Bureau's operation, making these records foundational for the county's earliest years.
substitute_sources.source: NARA, Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, RG 105.

substitute_sources.description: Walker Evans's photographs and James Agee's text in 'Let Us Now Praise Famous Men' (1941) document specific Hale County sharecropper families in the mid-1930s with extraordinary detail.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Congress (Evans photographs); published book widely available
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The families documented by Agee and Evans — the Gudger, Woods, and Ricketts families (pseudonyms) — have been identified by subsequent researchers. If an ancestor connects to these families, the Agee-Evans documentation provides unparalleled detail about daily life, material conditions, and family structure.
substitute_sources.source: Agee, James, and Walker Evans. Let Us Now Praise Famous Men. Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1941.

narrative_hooks: Hale County was born during Reconstruction — {ancestor_name}'s pre-1867 records are at Eutaw (Greene County) or Linden (Marengo County), not Greensboro.

Walker Evans photographed Hale County families in 1936 with a documentary precision that exceeds any census or courthouse record — if {ancestor_name} connects to those families, the Library of Congress holds images of the cabin, the field, and the faces.

Hale County's courthouse has stood since 1908, and the records it holds begin in 1867 — the year of the county's creation and the year Reconstruction began rewriting the political landscape of the Black Belt.

The 1867 boundary that created Hale County was drawn during Reconstruction — for {ancestor_name}, the county line itself was a political document, drawn by a government that was trying to rebuild a region that slavery had defined.
source_references: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.
Agee, James, and Walker Evans. Let Us Now Praise Famous Men. Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1941.
National Archives and Records Administration. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, Record Group 105.
